mnemonics.title: 8 Wrapper Classes Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: I LoVe FDoS (I Love Fossil Dinosaurs) - Integer, Long, Void(not), Float, Double, Short (FDoS). For remaining: Byte, Character, Boolean. Or: "I Luv Fresh Danish Sweets Bought Cheaply" = Integer, Long, Float, Double, Short, Byte, Character, Boolean.

mnemonics.title: Cache Range -128 to 127
mnemonics.content: Think of it as -128 to 127 equals -2^8 to 2^8-1 (one byte range)

mnemonics.title: Wrapper Immutable - Keep Safe Value Inside
mnemonics.content: K-Safe: KEEP WRAPPERS SAFE = Wrappers Are Immutable, Thread-safe, Fixed, Efficient, Secure

acronyms.expansion: Basic Object Wrappers - Integer, Long, Float, Double, Short, Byte, Character, Boolean
acronyms.usage: Remember the 8 fundamental wrapper classes in Java

acronyms.expansion: Integer Short, Equals valueOf, equals, Unboxing - Important concepts
acronyms.usage: Key concepts in Type Wrappers: Integer cache, equals() vs ==, Autoboxing/Unboxing

visualTips.visualization: Draw an inverted triangle: Object at top, Number below it, then two branches - Character on left, Boolean on right, and numeric wrappers (Byte, Short, Integer, Long, Float, Double) below Number on the right. This shows Character and Boolean don't extend Number directly (actually Character does extend Number but conceptually separated).

Draw a two-way arrow: Primitive Types (left) <--> Wrapper Classes (right). Label top arrow 'Autoboxing' pointing right (int → Integer), bottom arrow 'Unboxing' pointing left (Integer → int). Add note: 'Java Compiler does this automatically!'

analogies.analogy: Think of a primitive value (like 10) as a raw gift, and the Wrapper class (Integer) as putting that gift in a beautiful box. You can now handle it like an object - put it in collections, pass it around, but when you need the actual gift, you 'unbox' it.

The Integer cache (-128 to 127) is like a library with pre-assigned shelves for commonly used book numbers. When you ask for book #50, you get the same pre-existing book. For book #500, the library creates a brand new copy each time you request.

A wrapper object is like a locked safe containing the primitive value. Unboxing is like using the key (compiler) to open the safe and retrieve the valuable primitive inside. If the safe is null (empty), trying to open it crashes (NPE).
